History
TEACHING MODULE
vi
Preface 
The History Teaching Module has been developed to guide a Teacher on how to deliver content 
in the 2024 History Syllabus.  The Module aims to facilitate the provision of quality education that 
is aligned with the Competence Based Curriculum. Furthermore, the module aims to support a 
Teacher to help learners attain the competences, knowledge, skills, values and positive attitudes 
that enable children to live and grow into productive and useful members of their communities 
and the Zambian society as a whole. 
The content/topics provided in this module are aimed at facilitating holistic development of 
learners. The suggested activities are designed to offer hands-on experiences through learner 
centered approaches. Thus, the learners will develop knowledge, desirable lifelong skills, values 
and positive attitudes needed for their personal and national development. 
It is hoped that the module will make learning History at Ordinary Level Secondary more 
meaningful and enjoyable as it is highly activity oriented. 
 
 
Kelvin Mambwe  (PhD)
Permanent Secretary- Educational Services
MINISTRY OF EDUCATION

Form 1 Term 2
TEACHING MODULE
vii
Acknowledgement
The History Teaching Module presented here is a result of broad-based consultation involving 
several stakeholders within and outside the education system.
We are grateful for the financial and technical support provided by teachers, Lecturers from 
Colleges of Education and Universities, Subject Associations, Civil Society Organisations and 
Cooperating Partners. Their invaluable input and expertise have enriched the content and quality 
of this teaching module.
We also acknowledge the dedication and hard work of the staff at the Curriculum Development 
Centre, whose tireless efforts ensured the successful completion of the Teaching Module 
development process.
Thank you all for your contributions.
 
Charles Ndakala (Dr.)
Director - Curriculum Development
MINISTRY OF EDUCATION

History
TEACHING MODULE
viii
Introduction
The History Form 1 Teaching Module has been prepared by the Ministry of Education under 
the Directorate of Curriculum Development to kick start the implementation of the 2024 
Competence Based Curriculum.    
The Module covers some of the topics offered at form 1 level, particularly in term 2. The module 
has been designed in alignment to the 2024 Competence Based Curriculum. The material covered 
is in full response to the demands and dictates of the new Curriculum.  The new curriculum is 
Competent Based and focuses on applied skills and knowledge that enable learners to successfully 
perform in professional, educational and other life contents. The Module is therefore, aimed at 
producing a learner that will be globally accepted based on their vast interpretation of the past, 
present and the future. 
The suggested learner activities are designed to offer hands-on experiences through learner 
centered approaches. 
Teachers are however, advised to explore more avenues of learner activities so as to maximise 
the exploitation of skills in learners. The module is not an end in itself. 
It is hoped that the module will make learning History at Ordinary Level Secondary more 
meaningful and enjoyable as it is highly activity oriented.

Form 1 Term 2
TEACHING MODULE
1
TOPIC 1.5: THE BEGINNING AND SPREAD OF IRON                  	
	
	
      WORKING AND FARMING
Introduction
The beginning of iron working and farming in the Middle East and its spread to Africa transformed 
the livelihoods of people in Zambia. This topic will discuss the development of iron technology 
and farming.
Hook: Imagine how life would be without farming and iron tools! How did farming 
start? Were iron tools always there since the beginning of human life? How did 
people farm before the invention of iron tools? How did people begin to make iron 
tools? We will answer some of these questions in this topic.
General Competences
 
 Analytical thinking
 
 Communication
 
 Creativity and Innovation
 
 Critical thinking
 
 Evaluation
 
 Digital
 
 Entrepreneurship
 
 Environmental sustainability
KEY WORDS
Agriculture:  refers to crop production and rearing of livestock 
Farming: refers growing of crops and rearing of animals
Fertile Crescent:  refers to an area between rivers Tigris and Euphrates in the Middle 
East where farming began in the world. 
Livestock: Domesticated animals like goats, pigs, chickens, cattle and many more 
Iron: refers to a mineral extracted from rocks used to farming tools
Barter system: refers to the exchange of goods for goods among the various groups of 
people in the past
SUB-TOPIC 1.5.1:  DEVELOPMENT OF IRON-TECHNOLOGY AND 
FARMING
This sub-topic explains the development of iron technology and farming. It will locate on the 
map early areas of farming and iron working, identify settlement areas of Iron Age Farmers in 
Zambia, relate Ing’ombe Ilede to the global trade environment and analyse the importance of 
iron technology.

History
TEACHING MODULE
2
 Specific Competence 1.1.5.1: Analyse impact of Iron technology in farming
 Learning Activities: 
 
 Locating on the map early areas of farming and Iron working
 
 Identifying settlement areas of Iron Age farmers in Zambia
 
 Relating Ing’ombe Ilede to the global trade environment
 
 Analysing the importance of iron technology
LEARNING ACTIVITY 1
Locating on the map early areas of farming and iron working
 Suggested teaching and learning materials 
 
 Map showing early areas of farming and iron working in the Middle East and Africa.
Teacher to conduct the following activities in order to help learners locate on the map 
early areas of farming and iron working in the Middle East and Africa
 
 Ask learners prior to the lesson to research on early areas of farming and iron working 
in the Middle East and Africa
 
 Ask learners to present their findings
 
 Display a map showing early areas of farming and iron working in the Middle East 
and Africa
 
 Ask learners to draw the map
 
 Consolidates using the map
In this activity, learners will locate on the map early areas of farming and iron working in 
the Middle East and Africa
CONTENT TIPS
Early Areas of Farming and Iron working in the Middle East
 
 Farming began around 10000 BC in the Middle East between rivers Tigris and Eu­
phrates known as Fertile Crescent or Mesopotamia.
 
 Iron working was started by the Hittites of Asia Minor (Turkey) who began the use 
of iron to make tools around 3000 B.C.
Early Areas of Farming and Iron working in Africa
 
 From the Middle East, knowledge of farming and iron working spread to Egypt 
along the Nile Valley around 650 B.C.
 
 In Africa, iron working began along the Nile Valley in Egypt and spread to Sudan 
(Kush) where an iron working centre was established at Meroe.
 
 The knowledge of iron working then spread further to Ethiopia (Axum).
In this learning activity, learners will locate on the map early areas of farming and iron 
working in the Middle East and Africa.

Form 1 Term 2
TEACHING MODULE
3
LEARNING ACTIVITY 2
 
Identifying settlement areas of Iron Age farmers in Zambia
Suggested teaching and learning materials
 
 Map showing settlement areas of Iron Age farmers in Zambia
Teacher to conduct the following activities to enable learners identify settlement areas of 
Iron Age farmers in Zambia
 
 Brainstorming: Ask learners to mention settlement areas of Iron Age farmers in 
Zambia
 
 Display a map showing settlement areas of Iron Age farmers in Zambia
 
 Ask learners to identify settlement areas of Iron Age farmers in Zambia
 
 Consolidate key points for the learners
 
 Instruct learners to draw the map
CONTENT TIPS
Settlement areas of Iron Age farmers in Zambia
 
 Kalundu and Isamu Pati in Kalomo District 
 
 Ing’ombe Ilede in Siavonga District 
LEARNING ACTIVITY 2
 
 Relating Ing’ombe Ilede to the global trade environment.
 Suggested teaching and learning materials
 
 Chart showing trade at Ing’ombe Ilede 
 
 A table showing trade imports and exports to relate Ing’ombe Ilede to the global 
trade environment.
Teacher to conduct the following activities to enable learners relate Ing’ombe Ilede to the 
global trade environment
 
 Display a chart showing trade at Ing’ombe Ilede
 
 Ask learners to study the chart
 
 Conduct a class discussion based on the chart 
 
 Ask learners to draw the diagram on the chart
 
 Ask learners to refer to the chart to complete the table
Complete the table below:
Items found at Ing’ombe 
Ilede
Items imported at 
Ing’ombe Ilede 
Items exported from 
Ing’ombe Ilede.

History
TEACHING MODULE
4
CONTENT TIPS
Relating Ing’ombe Ilede to the Global Trade Environment
Ing’ombe Ilede was an Early Iron Age trading centre located near the confluence of 
Zambezi and Lusitu rivers in Siavonga District, Southern Province. 
 
 The people of Ing’ombe Ilede possessed salt and ivory
 
 They imported gold and ivory from the south as far as Zimbabwe
 
 Copper, slaves, ivory and iron objects were imported from the north as far as 
Katanga
 
 These items attracted foreign traders from Asia and the Middle East
 
 These traders came to Ing’ombe Ilede from the east coast using the Zambezi River
 
 They brought iron objects, beads, sea shells and cloth
 
 This led to the integration of Ing’ombe Ilede into the international trade environ­
ment
 
 Similarly, modern Zambia has natural resources which enables it to be involved in 
global trade
 
 Zambia exports goods such as minerals, maize and electricity to other countries
 
 It also imports goods like oil, machinery, foodstuffs, and other goods
In this lesson learners will relate Ing’ombe Ilede to the global trade environment.
                            
LEARNING ACTIVITY 4
Analysing the importance of iron technology
Suggested teaching and learning materials
 
 Pictures of Iron Age tools used in agriculture, fishing, hunting and trading or
 
 Actual iron tools
The following activities should be carried out by the teacher to enable learners analyse 
the importance of iron technology
 
 Ask learners to mention the Iron Age tools used in agriculture, fishing, hunting 
and trading
 
 Consolidate learners’ responses using pictures
 
 Divide learners in groups
 
 Ask learners in groups to analyse the importance of iron technology in agriculture, 
fishing, hunting, trading and construction
 
 Ask group representatives to make presentations to the class
 
 Write the main points on the board
 
 In this learning activity learners will analyse the importance of iron technology
CONTENT TIPS
The importance of Iron technology
 
 Agriculture: Iron technology led to increased food production. Iron tools like hoes, 
axes and picks enabled early farmers to cultivate large portions of land.

Form 1 Term 2
TEACHING MODULE
5
 
 Increased Population: Increased food production led to population increase
 
 Fishing:  Iron technology led to increased improved fishing using iron tools such 
as spears and hooks.
 
 Hunting: Iron technology improved hunting through use of spears and arrows made 
of iron. In modern times guns made of iron components are used to hunt animals 
by those with licences
 
 Trading:  Black smith make iron tools such as hoes, axes, slashers, cutters for sale 
on the market. Metal fabrication industries also make modern iron equipment and 
tools for sale for revenue generation.
 
 Construction: Iron is used in roofing houses, making window frames, building 
poles and others to establishment permanent infrastructure
 
 Migration: Iron tools encouraged people to migrate into new areas of settlement
Expected standard
Importance of iron technology analysed correctly  
SUMMARY
The beginning of ion working and farming changed the ways of life of the people in 
different societies. This is because it led to improved trade, food production, hunting, 
construction, migration and others. 
ASSESSMENT
 
(Assessments can be done during and after each learning activity)
1.	 What challenges would early and modern farmers have faced if Iron-Technology had 
not been discovered? 
2.	 You have observed a reduction in food production in your community. Using the 
knowledge of iron technology and farming, how would you promote crop production 
in your local community?                                                                                                              
3.	 Many young people in Zambia today have remained jobless even after acquiring 
tertiary education. Explain how young people can utilise iron technology to earn a 
living.

History
TEACHING MODULE
6
TOPIC: 1.6 THE BANTU SPEAKING PEOPLE 
Introduction
The Bantu speaking people are a diverse group of people who have similarities in languages, 
culture and religious beliefs. This topic discusses the early Bantu settlement in Africa, Bantu 
migrations into Zambia, decentralised and centralised societies in pre-colonial Zambia.
Hook: What language is the word ‘Bantu?’ Some people say that the Bantu 
are only found in Zambia. This topic will answer a lot of unanswered 
questions about the Bantu people.
General Competences
 
 Analysis
 
 Communication
 
 Citizenship
 
 Critical thinking 
 
 Digital
KEY  WORDS
Bantu: refers to a group of Africans found south of the Sahara who have similar 
languages and culture.
Migration: the movement of people from one place to another in search of food or 
better living conditions.
Benue Region: the region in West Africa where the Bantu Speaking people are believed 
to have originated from.
Sub-Saharan Africa: the area of Africa located south of the Saharan desert where the 
Bantu people migrated and settled.
Settlement: the act of establishing communities in new areas.
Paramount chief: a chief who has overall control over sub-chiefs.
Tribute: a show of loyalty to the traditional leaders through giving items and 
labour.	
SUB-TOPIC:1.6.1: EARLY BANTU SETTLEMENT IN AFRICA
This sub-topic will discuss the term Bantu. It will also identify the Bantu speaking people and 
locate on the map their early settlement in West Africa. It will further outline reasons for early 
Bantu migrations from West Africa and analyse the course of the migrations. 
Specific Competence 1.6.1.1:
Locate origins of the Bantu Speaking people of Africa
Learning Activities

Form 1 Term 2
TEACHING MODULE
7
 
 Discussing the term, Bantu.
 
 Identifying the Bantu speaking people.
 
 Locating on the map of Africa, early Bantu settlement in West Africa.
 
 Outlining reasons for early Bantu migration from West Africa.
 
 Analysing the course of the early Bantu Migrations from the West Africa.
LEARNING ACTIVITY 1
Discussing the term Bantu
Suggested teaching and learning materials
Flash cards bearing the word “person”
Teacher should conduct the following activities with learners in order to discuss the 
term Bantu 
 
 Divide learners in groups
 
 Distribute flash cards bearing the word “person”
 
 Instruct learners to discuss and write the word “person” in different languages
 
 Ask learners to analyse similarities in the words
 
 Ask group representatives to present their findings to the class
 
 Write and consolidate the points from the learners
In this activity learners will discover that some words are similar among the Bantu 
speaking people. For example, the word “person” had similar pronunciations from 
different languages that learners used.
CONTENT TIPS
 
 The Bantu are people who speak similar languages
 
 For example, the word “person” has the root “ntu” and the prefix “mu” in various 
Bantu languages 
 
 The term Bantu refers to a large family of languages spoken across central Eastern 
and Southern Africa
LEARNING ACTIVITY 2
Identifying the Bantu speaking people
Suggested teaching and learning materials
Map showing some groups of people found in Africa
Activity 1: Identifying the Bantu Speaking People
The following activities should be carried out by the teacher to enable learners identi­
fy the Bantu speaking people 
 
 Prior to this lesson, ask learners to carry out a research about the Bantu speaking 
people

History
TEACHING MODULE
8
 
 Divide learners into groups
 
 Distribute maps showing groups of people found in Africa
 
 Ask learners to identify the Bantu speaking people from the maps
 
 Instruct group representatives to report their findings to the class
 
 Write the points on the board.
In this activity, learners will be able to identify the Bantu speaking people in Africa
CONTENT TIPS
There are many groups of Bantu speaking people in Africa	
For example, Luba, Lunda, Zulu, Ndebele, Sukuma, Shona, Yao, Kikuyu and many others
LEARNING ACTIVITY 3
Locating on the map of Africa early Bantu settlement in West Africa
Suggested teaching and learning materials
Blank map of Africa
The following activity should be carried by the teacher to help learners locate the early Bantu 
settlement in West Africa
 
 Divide learners into small groups
 
 Distribute blank maps of Africa
 
 Ask each group to locate the early Bantu settlement in West Africa on the blank 
map provided
 
 Instruct learners to mark the area settled by Bantu-speaking people in West Africa
 
 Ask groups to share their completed maps with the class
 
 Display the map showing the Benue Valley in West Africa
In this learning activity, learners will discover the location of the early Bantu settlement in 
West Africa.
CONTENT TIPS
 
 The early Bantu settled in West Africa in the Benue Region located between Lake 
Chad and the Cameroon Highlands.
LEARNING ACTIVITY 4
Outlining reasons for early Bantu migrations from West Africa

Form 1 Term 2
TEACHING MODULE
9
Suggested teaching and learning material
Document containing information explaining reasons for the Bantu migrations from 
West Africa
The following activities should be conducted by the teacher in order for learners to 
outline reasons for early Bantu migrations from West Africa;
 
 Ask learners to work in groups
 
 Distribute documents explaining reasons for the Bantu migrations from West 
Africa
 
 Ask learners to study the document 
 
 Tell learners to discuss the reasons for the Bantu migrations from West Africa
 
 Ask group representatives to report their findings to the class
 
 Write the main points on the board
 
 Instruct the learners to copy the points in their books
In this activity learners will outline the reasons for the Bantu migrations from West 
Africa 
CONTENT TIPS
Reasons for the Bantu migrations from West Africa
 
 Population increase
 
 Search for fertile land
 
 Drought and climate change
 
 Conflicts and wars
 
 Diseases
 
 Love for adventure and exploration
 
 Iron working technology
 
 Expansion of trade
 
 Witchcraft
 
 Slave trade
 
 Succession disputes
 
 Family quarrels
LEARNING ACTIVITY 5
Analysing the course of the early Bantu Migrations from West Africa
Suggested teaching and learning material
Map of Africa showing the routes of the Bantu migrations from West Africa
The following activities should be conducted by the teacher to enable learners analyse 
the course of the Bantu migrations from West Africa:
 
 Display a map on the course of the Bantu migrations from West Africa

History
TEACHING MODULE
10
 
 Ask learners to study the map
 
 Guide learners to discuss the course of the Bantu migrations from West Africa
 
 Summarise the main points 
In this activity learners will analyse the course of the Bantu migrations from West Africa
CONTENT TIPS
The course of the Bantu migrations from West Africa
 
 The Central route: movements from West Africa into the Congo Basin forming the 
Luba and Lunda kingdoms
 
 The Eastern route: movements from West Africa into the Great Lakes region such the 
Baganda, Banyoro, Basoga, and others proceeded southwards such as Tonga, Shona 
and Nguni groups 
 
 The Western route: movement from West Africa into Southern Africa such as Ovambo, 
Okavango, Herero. Some settled in Angola, Namibia
Expected standard: origins of the Bantu Speaking people in Africa located correctly
SUMMARY
This sub-topic has discussed the term Bantu, identified the Bantu speaking people located the 
early Bantu settlement in West Africa. It has also outlined the reasons for Bantu migrations from 
West Africa and analysed the course of the Bantu migrations
SUB-TOPIC1.6.2: BANTU MIGRATIONS INTO ZAMBIA
The Bantu migrations were one of the most significant movements of people in African History. 
This sub-topic will discuss the causes for movement of Bantu-speaking people into Zambia. 
These migrations shaped the linguistic, cultural and social structures of modern day Zambia.
Specific Competence 1.6.2.1: Use knowledge of common origins of Zambian people to 
promote national unity
Learning Activities
 
 Discussing causes for movement of various Bantu Speaking groups into Zambia.
 
 Discussing the course of Bantu migrations into Zambia.
 
 Drawing a map of Zambia showing historical settlements for Bantu Speaking people.
 
 Analysing the effects of Bantu migrations on national unity.
 
 Using shared heritage to foster national unity in Zambia.
LEARNING ACTIVITY 1

Form 1 Term 2
TEACHING MODULE
11
 Discussing causes for movement of various Bantu Speaking groups into Zambia.
Suggested learning and teaching material
Chart showing causes for migrations in modern day Zambia
 
 Chart showing causes for Bantu migrations
The following activities should be carried out by the teacher in order for learners to 
discuss the causes for Bantu migrations into Zambia:
 
 Ask learners to draw table showing causes for migrations in modern day Zambia and 
complete it
 
 Display a chart showing causes of the Bantu migrations into Zambia
 
 Ask learners to classify the causes into political, economic and social categories as 
shown in the table
 
 Conduct a whole class discussion to consolidate the causes of the Bantu migrations
 
 Instruct learners to write the consolidated points 
 
 Causes for migrations in modern society
OPPORTUNITIES
CHALLENGES
Causes for Bantu migrations into Zambia
POLITICAL
ECONOMIC
SOCIAL
 In this activity learners will analyse the political, economic and social causes for the Bantu 
migrations into Zambia.
CONTENT TIPS
Causes for the Bantu migrations into Zambia:
From Katanga
 
 Conflict over land
 
 Expansion of powerful kingdoms
 
 Internal conflicts and wars
 
 Succession disputes
 
 Shortage of water resources
 
 Desire to receive tribute and control trade
 
 Discovery and use of iron technology
 
 Need for fertile land

History
TEACHING MODULE
12
 
 Shortage of pasture for animals
 
 Social causes
 
 Adventure
 
 Family quarrels
 
 Overpopulation in their original homeland
 
 Witchcraft
From South Africa
Effects of Mfecane
 
 The wars of Shaka led to a wave of wars and forced migrations across southern 
Africa. Many groups like the Ngoni, Ndebele and the Kololo migrated to escape Zulu 
military expansion
LEARNING ACTIVITY 2
Discussing the course of Bantu migrations into Zambia
Suggested teaching and learning materials
Map showing the course of the Bantu migrations into Zambia from the Great Lakes Re­
gion, Katanga and Southern Africa
The following activities should be conducted by the teacher to enable learners to discuss 
the course of the Bantu migrations into Zambia.
 
 Display the map showing the course of Bantu migrations into Zambia
 
 Ask learners to draw the map 
 
 Instruct learners to study the map to trace migration routes of the Bantu speaking 
groups into Zambia 
 
 Whole class discussion on the course of the Bantu migrations into Zambia
 In this activity, learners will discuss the course of the Bantu migrations into Zambia
CONTENT TIPS
The course of the Bantu migrations into Zambia highlights the resilience of early 
communities and their ability to adapt to new environments
 
 Movement from the Great Lakes Region
 
 The Bantu who migrated in to this group include the Tonga, Lungu and Tabwa. 
They moved from East Africa and settled Zambia.
 
 Movement from Katanga
 
 From Luba include: Bemba, Chewa, Soli, Lenje, Lala, Lamba, Tumbuka and many 
more
 
 From Lunda include: Lozi, Lunda, Luvale, Chokwe, Mbunda, Kaonde and many
 
 Movement from South Africa
 
 This included the Ngoni and Kololo
LEARNING ACTIVITY 3
Drawing a map of Zambia showing historical settlements for Bantu Speaking people.

Form 1 Term 2
TEACHING MODULE
13
Suggested teaching and learning material
Map of Zambia 
The teacher should carry out the following activity to enable learners draw a map of 
Zambia showing historical settlements of the Bantu speaking people
 
 Display the map showing historical settlements for Bantu speaking people
 
 Ask learners to draw the map in their books
 
 Consolidates the information on the map
In this activity learners will draw the map of Zambia showing historical settlements for 
Bantu speaking people in Zambia.
CONTENT TIPS
The Historical settlement for Bantu speaking people in Zambia
 
 Central province- Lala, Lenje, Swaka, Sala and many more
 
 Eastern province – Chewa, Chikunda, Nsenga, Ngoni, Senga, Tumbuka and many 
more
 
 Luapula province –Lunda, Kabende, Ushi, Chishinga and many more
 
 Muchinga province – Bemba, Bisa, Lambia, Namwanga and many more
 
 Northern province – Bemba, Mambwe, Lungu,
 
 North-western – Lunda, Luvale, Chokwe, Kaonde, Luchazi, Mbunda, Ndembu and 
many more 
 
 Southern province – Tonga, Ila, Toka-leya, Ila and many more 
 
 Western province – Lozi, Nkoya, Subiya, Nyengo, Kwangwa, Mbunda and many 
more
 
 Lusaka province- Soli, Lenje
 
 Copperbelt province- Lamba
 
LEARNING ACTIVITY 4
Analysing the effects of Bantu migrations on national unity
Suggested teaching and learning material
 
 Chart showing effects of the Bantu migrations on national unity
The teacher should carry out the following activities to enable learners to analyse the 
effects of the Bantu migrations on national unity
 
 Ask learners prior to this lesson to find out if the Bantu migrations have contributed 
to national unity
 
 Divide learners into two groups to debate the motion: Bantu migrations brought 
national unity in Zambia
 
 Summarise the debate by analysing the effects of Bantu migrations on national unity. 
Teacher must emphasise that the Bantu migrations have contributed to national unity 
In this activity learners will analyse the effects of the Bantu migrations on national 
unity.

History
TEACHING MODULE
14
CONTENT TIPS
Effects of the Bantu migrations on national unity
 
 Shared heritage – common identity promotes national unity. 
 
 Cultural exchange – as different communities settled they shared customs, traditions 
and values that fostered national unity.
 
 Diverse ethnic groups share same historical experiences based on common origins. 
This is a unifying factor.
 
 Pre-colonial political structures provided early models of leadership and community 
cohesion that later influenced modern state-building.
 
 Intermarriages and social bonds help break ethnic barriers leading to stronger social 
bonds that contribute to national unity.
 
 The need to coexist in a shared territory prompts new social norms and practices.
 
 Migrating groups brought with them new innovations in agriculture, iron working 
and trade which contributed to a shared economy.
LEARNING ACTIVITY 5
 Using shared heritage to foster national unity in Zambia
Suggested teaching and learning materials
Pictures depicting shared heritage (such as ceremonies, traditional attire, languages)
The teacher should conduct the following activities in order to enable learners use shared 
heritage to foster national unity in Zambia
 
 Circulate pictures depicting shared heritage.
 
 Ask learners to study the pictures
 
 Ask learners to analyse how the pictures depict the use of shared heritage to foster 
national unity
 
 Whole class discussion
 
 Consolidates and writes the main points
 
 In this activity learners will learn to use shared heritage to foster national unity
CONTENT TIPS
 
 Shared heritage refers to common cultural, historical and social elements that different 
ethnic groups in Zambia have.
 
 Examples of shared heritage in Zambia include ceremonies, traditional attire, food, 
songs, shrines and others.
 
 They help to foster appreciation and understanding of each other’s culture.
 
 Zambian societies emphasise values such as respect, hospitality and communal living 
which are shared across different ethnic groups.
 
 Proverbs and folk tales from different tribes often carry similar messages about unity

Form 1 Term 2
TEACHING MODULE
15
and cooperation
 
 Language as a unifying factor: some local languages such as Bemba, Nyanja Tonga 
and Lozi are widely spoken across different regions
 
 Intermarriages and social integration.
 
 Shared heritage fosters national unity by promoting mutual respect, encouraging 
patriotism, building peace, social cohesion and strengthening national pride.
Expected standard: knowledge of common origins of Zambian people used to promote na­
tional unity accordingly
SUMMARY
This sub-topic has discussed the causes and course for migrations of the Bantu Speaking 
groups into Zambia, illustrated the historical settlement of the Bantu speaking people 
using a map of Zambia and analysed the effects of Bantu migrations on national unity. It 
has also shown how shared heritage is used to foster national unity in Zambia.
SUB - TOPIC 1.6.3: 
DECENTRALISED SOCIETIES IN PRE-COLONIAL ZAMBIA.
Decentralised societies in pre-colonial Zambia were communities or ethnic groups that did not 
have a centralised form of government. These societies were organised around clans, lineages, 
or family groups, with leadership distributed across elders or village heads. This subtopic will 
identify and locate on the map of Zambia decentralised societies in areas of settlement. It will 
also analyse key features of decentralised societies.
Specific competence 1.6.3.1: Analyse features of decentralised societies
Learning Activities
 
 Identifying decentralised societies in Zambia
 
 Locating on the map of Zambia areas of settlement for decentralised societies
 
 Analysing key features of decentralised societies in general.
LEARNING ACTIVITY 1
Identifying decentralised societies in Zambia.
Suggested teaching and learning material
Chart showing decentralised societies in Zambia
The following activities should be conducted by the teacher to enable the learners identify 
decentralised societies in Zambia
 
 Display chart showing decentralised societies in Zambia
 
 Ask learners to study the dsplayed chart
 
 Consolidates the points on decentralised societies
 
 Ask learners to write the list in their books 
In this activity learners will identify the decentralised societies in Zambia.

History
TEACHING MODULE
16
CONTENT TIPS
These were societies that did not have a strong central government and were not ruled 
by kings or chiefs.
Examples of decentralised societies in Zambia include the Tonga, Ila, Lenje, Sala, Soli 
and others.

Locating on the map of Zambia areas of settlement for decentralised societies.

In this activity learners will locate the decentralised societies on the map of Zambia
CONTENT TIPS
Areas of settlement for decentralised societies in Zambia
 
 Southern province – Tonga, Ila
 
 Central province – Lenje, Sala
 
 Lusaka province - Soli
 
LEARNING ACTIVITY 3
 Analysing key features of decentralised societies in general.
Suggested teaching and learning materials
Document containing information on features of decentralised societies 
The following activities should be conducted by the teacher in order to enable learners 
analyse key features of decentralised societies in general
 
 Divide the class into groups
 
 Distribute the documents containing information on features of decentralised societies
 
 Ask learners to study the documents
 
 Instruct learners to discuss and write the features of decentralised societies
 
 Ask group representatives to present their findings to the class 
 
 In this activity learners will analyse the key features of decentralised societies
CONTENT TIPS
Key features of decentralised societies in Zambia
 
 Loose political structure – they did not have chiefs and kings, leadership was

Form 1 Term 2
TEACHING MODULE
17
distributed among village elders, clan leaders or family heads.
 
 They were mainly farmers, who grew crops such as millet and sorghum and kept 
livestock such as cattle goats, sheep and pigs. They also reared chickens.
 
 They were involved in local trade.
 
 They did not have organised military structure and this exposed them to attack from 
powerful groups.
 
 Societies were organised around extended families with strong kinship ties. 
Ceremonies, traditions and oral history played a role in maintaining cultural identity 
and governance.
Expected standard: features of decentralised societies analysed accordingly
SUMMARY
In this sub-topic, we have identified and analysed the key features of decentralised 
societies. We have also located the decentralised societies on the map of Zambia.
SUB-TOPIC 1.6.4: CENTRALISED SOCIETIES
Centralised societies are societies that have a strong central government and are led either 
by a chief, king or queen. This sub-topic will identify the centralised societies in pre-
colonial Zambia, locate the centralised societies on the map and analyse their key features 
in general. It will also show how features of centralised societies are integrated in modern 
Zambia.                                                                                                                                                                                           
Specific Competence 1.6.4.1: Integrate features of centralised societies in modern Zambi­
an society.
Learning Activities: 
 
 Identifying centralised societies in pre-colonial Zambia 
 
 Locating centralised societies on the map of Zambia 
 
 Analysing key features of centralised societies in general
 
 Integrating features of centralised societies in modern Zambia

 Identifying centralised societies in pre-colonial Zambia

In this activity, learners will locate areas of settlement of the centralised societies on

CONTENT TIPS
Centralised societies in pre-colonial Zambia
 
 Bemba kingdom – located in northern Zambia ruled by the king known as chief 
Chitimukulu.
 
 The Lozi kingdom - located in western Zambia ruled by the Litunga.
 
 The Lunda kingdom – located in the Luapula region led by Chief Mwata Kazembe.
 
 The Chewa kingdom – located in the Eastern region led by Chief Gawa Undi
 
 The Ngoni kingdom – located in Eastern Zambia led by Chief Mpezeni

Analyse key features of centralised societies in general

ers analyse key features of centralised societies in general

 Instruct learners to discuss and write the features of centralised societies
 
 In this activity learners will analyse the key features of centralised societies in 
general.

Form 1 Term 2
TEACHING MODULE
19
CONTENT TIPS
The following are the features of centralised societies:
 
 In a centralised society, chiefs had centralised political power. 
 
 The Chief was helped to rule by the senior chiefs, sub-chiefs and Village headmen.
 
 Chiefs controlled trade routes for salt, ivory, copper and slaves.
 
 All the centralised societies received tributes in form of food, labour and goods 
from their subjects and the conquered people. 
 
 Chiefs also organised traditional ceremonies which brought all the people within the 
kingdom together. For example, Ukusefya pa Ngwena (Bemba), Kuomboka(Lozi), 
Ncwala(Ngoni), 
Umutomboko 
(Lunda), 
Likumbi 
Lya 
Mize(Luvale) 
Kulamba(Chewa).
 
 Centralised societies had well organised army to defend the kingdom and expand 
territories. For example, the Ngoni had a warrior based system.
LEARNING ACTIVITY 4
 
Integrating features of centralised societies in modern Zambia
Suggested teaching and learning material
 
 Chart showing features of centralised societies in modern society
 
 Pictures showing features of  centralised societies in modern Zambian society
The teacher should conduct the following activities in order to help learners show 
how features of centralised societies have been integrated in modern Zambian society
 
 Divide learners into groups
 
 Ask learners to draw the table 
 
 Ask learners to complete   the table by showing how features of centralised societies 
have been integrated in the modern Zambian society.
 
 Ask learners to present their work to the class
 
 Display a complete chart showing how features of centralised societies have been 
integrated in the modern Zambian society.
 
 Ask learners to copy the completed table in their books. 
Features
Centralised societies
 Modern Zambian   society 
Centralised political power
Monopoly of trade
Tribute system
Traditional ceremonies
Strong military structure
 In this activity learners will show how features of centralised societies have been integrated in 
modern Zambian society.

History
TEACHING MODULE
20
CONTENT TIPS
Features of centralised societies have been integrated in modern Zambian society in 
the following ways:
Centralised political power 
Zambia being a republic has an elected president who is helped to rule by ministers, 
members of parliament, councilors and district commissioners. This clearly shows how 
the Zambian system of governance functions similarly to chiefs and paramount chiefs. 
Zambian government has embraced traditional leadership through the House of Chiefs 
established in 1965 to date with 50 members. Chiefs have continued to be in charge of 
traditional affairs and offer advice the president on traditional matters.
Monopoly of trade
The government manages economies through trade regulations and public services. 
Tribute system
The government also collects taxes from its citizens through the Zambia Revenue 
Authority and distributes to different sectors or areas of needy to foster development. 
This can be likened to the tribute system of pre-colonial Zambia.
Traditional ceremonies
In pre-colonial Zambia, traditional leaders used traditional ceremonies to unite people. 
Similarly in modern Zambia, successive governments have been using traditional 
ceremonies to foster national unity.
Organised military structure 
Centralised societies had organised armies to maintain stability. Modern societies rely 
on defense forces and police for national security. Like the Chiefs and Kings in pre-
colonial Zambia, the President of Zambia is the commander of the armed forces.
SUMMARY
This sub-topic has identified centralised societies in pre-colonial Zambia, located 
centralised societies on the map of Zambia and analysed key features of centralised 
societies in general. It has also shown how features of centralised societies have been 
integrated in modern Zambian society.
ASSESSMENT
1.	 In most of the Pre-colonial Centralised societies of Zambia, the Chief controlled the 
Army. Why is it an important aspect of every society including modern Zambia to 
have an army centrally controlled?
2.	 Most Pre-colonial societies in Zambia were located in specific regions of the country. 
However, people have now changed and different ethnic groups are now settling in 
any part of the country. What factors have contributed to this change in settlement 
patterns? 
3.	 How can historical awareness contribute to fostering a sense of understanding, respect 
and unity among various ethnic and cultural groups within Zambia?

Form 1 Term 2
TEACHING MODULE
21
SUMMARY
This topic has located the early Bantu settlements in Africa, discussed the Bantu 
migrations into Zambia and identified decentralised societies in pre-colonial Zambia. It 
has also analysed   key features of decentralised and centralised societies.
